goal: Controlled launch cohort from the 12 research/data pins (commit f0f79ed) optimized for outbound clicks. Baseline: 1,466 impressions / 21 outbound clicks per 30d; 66 historical pins avg 3.316% CTR; concrete numbers outperform vague titles.
note: 6 of 6 selected. The original protein-days-priced-dry-goods image (typo 'Gogds') was replaced 2026-07-13 by an owner-approved regeneration (gpt-image-2, $0.005) with headline '5 Protein Days Priced: From 82 Cents to $9.97'. Verified at full resolution and 236px mobile preview: spelling correct, no receipts/labels/menus/text cards, headline margins 5.7%/2.8%, portrait 1024x1536.

candidates.original_title: Beans: 98g Protein Per Dollar. Chicken Legs: 50g. Eggs: 34g.
candidates.final_title: Beans: 98g of Protein Per Dollar. Chicken Legs: 50g. Eggs: 34g.
candidates.description: We priced 49 foods with USDA protein data and July 2026 store prices. Dried pinto beans deliver 98 grams of protein per dollar, chicken drumsticks 50, eggs 34, bacon 9. See the full cheap protein ranking plus a week of budget meals built from the top three.
candidates.alt_text: A jar of dried beans, chicken drumsticks, and eggs with a chalkboard showing protein-per-dollar numbers 98, 50, 34.
candidates.hypothesis: Three hard numbers in the title (direct ranking result) beat a soft promise for driving clicks to the pillar.

candidates.original_title: The Only 3 Foods You Need for High Protein on a Budget
candidates.final_title: The Only 3 Foods You Need for High Protein on a Budget
candidates.description: Dried beans, eggs, and chicken drumsticks cover a week of high protein meals for a few dollars a day. Real July 2026 prices, USDA protein numbers, and a simple 7-day budget meal rotation you can copy tonight.
candidates.alt_text: A bowl of pinto beans, roasted chicken drumsticks, and two fried eggs beside a handwritten weekly meal plan.
candidates.hypothesis: A simplicity promise (only 3 foods) beats hard numbers for saves and clicks on the same pillar destination.

candidates.original_title: Build Your Day From the Dry Goods Aisle: 30g Fiber for 62 Cents
candidates.final_title: Build Your Day From the Dry Goods Aisle: 30g of Fiber for 62 Cents
candidates.description: The cheapest way to hit 30 grams of fiber a day is the dry goods aisle: beans, lentils, oats, and rice for 62 cents. See the meal-by-meal math from USDA fiber data and real store prices, plus four pricier days for comparison.
candidates.alt_text: Cloth bags of dried black beans and lentils with measuring cups, a bowl of cooked rice, and a notebook of fiber math on a wooden counter.
candidates.hypothesis: A constructive how-to framing (build your day) beats price-shock comparison for clicks to the fiber study.

candidates.original_title: That Restaurant Fiber Meal Costs $14.42. The Same Fiber at Home Costs 62 Cents.
candidates.final_title: That Restaurant Fiber Meal Costs $14.42. The Same Fiber at Home Costs 62 Cents.
candidates.description: A day of 30 grams of fiber costs $14.42 from restaurants or 62 cents from the dry goods aisle. Same fiber, 23x the price. See all five days priced meal by meal with USDA data and real menus.
candidates.alt_text: Split view of a restaurant receipt totaling $14.42 beside paper bags of dried beans and oats with a 62 cent price card and a quarter.
candidates.hypothesis: Price-shock comparison (restaurant vs home) beats constructive how-to for clicks to the fiber study.

candidates.original_title: Stop Paying $10 for Protein When It Costs Less Than a Dollar
candidates.final_title: Stop Paying $10 for Protein When It Costs Less Than a Dollar
candidates.description: Hitting 50 grams of protein costs 82 cents from the dry goods aisle or $9.97 from the drive-thru. Same protein, 12x the price. See all five days priced meal by meal with USDA protein data and July 2026 prices.
candidates.alt_text: Jars of dried beans and a carton of eggs on a kitchen counter beside a McDonald's takeout bag by a window.
candidates.hypothesis: Drive-thru vs pantry price contrast drives the highest outbound CTR for the protein day-cost study.

candidates.original_title: How to Get 50g of Protein Before Lunch for Under $1
candidates.alt_text: Meal-prepped beans, scrambled eggs in a pan, and a glass of milk on a wooden counter by a window.
candidates.rejection_reason: CLAIM MISMATCH baked into the image text: the article's $0.82 covers a FULL day (52.7g across breakfast, lunch, dinner, snack), not '50g before lunch'. Publishing this repeats the exact bait-pin failure that got the previous queue deleted. Best-photographed asset of the 12; regenerate the same scene with a supported claim, e.g. '50g of Protein a Day for 82 Cents'.

candidates.alt_text: A wooden cutting board with coins, a calculator, a grocery receipt, dried beans, eggs, and chicken legs.
candidates.rejection_reason: The '$30/Month' figure appears nowhere in the destination article (grep verified). Description's 'The USDA tested 49 foods' is also false attribution (we priced them using USDA data). Secondary: raw chicken foreground, calculator reading '3000', busy composition.

candidates.alt_text: A kitchen counter with 49 food labels arranged in a grid and glass jars of beans and lentils.
candidates.rejection_reason: The 49-card grid is AI text soup on zoom: 'KAROTI', 'CINEEOPS', 'CHIEKY BREAST', 'LIVICA', LENTILS repeated 4 times, QUINOA 4 times. Unreadable at mobile size, gibberish at full size. Classic generic-AI-advertising look.

candidates.original_title: A Day of Fiber, Priced Per Meal: From 62 Cents to $14.42
candidates.alt_text: Five meal representations with price tags, ranging from simple bean dishes to restaurant plates.
candidates.rejection_reason: Ten AI-garbled number cards ('6/6', '2/6', '223', '3.28', '2.57') that map to nothing in the article. Too many competing numbers, none credible. Title itself is fine; image fails.

candidates.original_title: What 30 Grams of Fiber Actually Costs (The Receipts)
candidates.alt_text: Paper receipt with grocery items beside a calculator showing fiber cost calculations.
candidates.rejection_reason: Typo baked into the headline: '(The Reciepts)'. Receipt subtotal $15.55 matches no day in the article (days are $0.62 / $1.74 / $4.18 / $14.42 / $1.99). Calculator keys are gibberish (MF, K+, MI, IIF). Credibility killer for a data pin.

candidates.original_title: The Protein Price Gap No One Talks About: $0.82 vs $9.97
candidates.alt_text: A grocery receipt next to a restaurant receipt with protein foods visible.
candidates.rejection_reason: Both receipts are walls of AI gibberish ('BUN IFOOD', 'FOEDERNET6', 'RESTAMD B CRNCS', 'HIL PIICVLE') occupying most of the frame. Raw chicken foreground. Title and numbers are excellent and traceable; the concept deserves a regeneration.

candidates.original_title: 5 Protein Days Priced: From the Dry Goods Aisle to the Drive-Thru (image REGENERATED 2026-07-13)
candidates.final_title: 5 Protein Days Priced: From 82 Cents to $9.97
candidates.description: Five real ways to hit 50 grams of protein in one day, each priced meal by meal: dry goods 82 cents, no-cook $2.05, meat-eater $2.78, realistic mixed $1.51, drive-thru $9.97. USDA protein data, July 2026 prices. See every meal and every number.
candidates.alt_text: Five meal prep containers in a row holding black beans, hard boiled eggs, diced chicken, and yogurt, ending with a plain brown takeout bag, under the headline 5 Protein Days Priced From 82 Cents to $9.97.
candidates.hypothesis: A direct number-range headline (82 cents to $9.97) beats the drive-thru comparison frame for outbound CTR on the same destination.

regeneration.replaced_defect: typo 'Dry Gogds Aisle' in original headline
